NR
Private brief · Mark Cunningham
Private brief · Prepared for Mark Cunningham · Rewritten May 2026

What AI answer engines actually cite when someone asks about banking in Hawaii.

00 Read this part first

Mark, this is the second version of this brief. The first version made a claim we tested and could not substantiate, and we have stopped selling the product that claim was built on. Before anything else in this document, the honest correction.

We used to ship a snippet you drop on your website that we claimed would get you cited by ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Google's AI Overviews. We had paying customers. Then we ran a real pre-registered test on our own domain and the result was zero citations. The crawlers the AI engines use do not execute JavaScript, which means our snippet was structurally invisible to them. We stopped selling that product and rebuilt the company around what does work, which is measurement.

The brief below describes what we do now, what we would measure for ASB, and what an engagement would look like. No predictions about citation lift. No promises about score jumps. Just observation, comparison, and the prepped guidance your team would use to act on it.

01 What we do now

We measure across two layers. Five engines that search the live web and cite their sources. Two engines that answer purely from model knowledge, meaning what AI says about you when it cannot look anything up. We watch all seven every day, in every category we run.

  • Citation-grade engines (5). Perplexity. ChatGPT with web search. Gemini with grounded search. Microsoft Copilot via Bing. Google AI Overviews via DataForSEO. These pull from the live web and surface their sources.
  • Model-knowledge engines (2). Claude. Gemma (Google's open-weight model, runnable by your compliance team for independent verification of our numbers). These answer from training data without searching, which is what a customer using Claude.ai or a Claude-powered support tool sees by default.

Both layers measure different failure modes. A brand invisible in citation is invisible when AI fact-checks itself. A brand invisible in model knowledge is invisible at the baseline, before any search happens. ASB needs the picture from both.

02 What this would look like for ASB

A research engagement against ASB would measure, every day, what each of the seven AI surfaces says when a real customer asks the questions that route to a banking decision. Examples of the queries we would track:

  • "Best bank in Honolulu for a small business loan"
  • "Who do I bank with in Hawaii if I just moved here"
  • "Best Hawaii bank for a first-time mortgage"
  • "American Savings Bank vs First Hawaiian Bank"
  • "Which Hawaii bank has the best business checking"
  • "Best Hawaii bank for a real estate investor"
  • "Hawaii bank for a startup"
  • "Most trusted bank in Hawaii"

For each query, on each engine, every day, we capture three things. The answer the AI gave. Which sources it cited. And whether ASB appeared in the answer at all. The output is not a dashboard you log into. It is a research memo your team reads.

03 How this is different from the tools you have heard of

The category already has tools. Profound and Peec are the well-funded ones. They sell a brand a dashboard with a score and a list of mentions. That works if your team has a dedicated marketing analyst whose job is to log in every day and interpret the surface.

We do not sell a dashboard. We hand you a forensic readout. Per query, per engine, per competitor, per source type. The memo reads like research, and it tells your team exactly what to change and in what order. The thing that no platform-internal dashboard will ever ship, because they cannot, is the cross-engine view paired with the cross-competitor view. Google will not tell ASB what Perplexity says. Perplexity will not tell ASB what ChatGPT cites. Only an outsider with no horse in the platform race can show you all of it and show you where your competitors sit.

04 The security story for a regulated bank

This is the part that usually surprises a bank's procurement team. We do not touch your systems.

  • No code on your property. Nothing to install. Nothing to review. Nothing to break.
  • No data flowing in from your side. ASB is the subject of measurement, not a data source. We do not need access to your CRM, your analytics, your customer records, or anything else.
  • No system integration. No API keys. No SSO. No OAuth. No read access to anything internal.

What we do is observe public AI answer engines from the outside, the same way a journalist or analyst would. That moves this from "technology vendor needing SOC 2, pen tests, and a six-month security review" to "external research vendor that needs an NDA and a basic vendor intake form." Closer to how a bank buys research from Forrester or competitive intelligence from a niche analyst than how it buys software.

The confidentiality that does matter, once we are producing a competitive readout that names ASB and its peers, is the handling of that document. We treat it as we would any client research. Aggregate patterns we observe across the broader dataset are never tied to a named customer.

05 The deliverable, and the boundary

For each engagement, your team receives a forensic memo and a prepped punch list. The memo names which queries, which engines, which competitors are being cited instead of ASB, which kinds of sources the AI is pulling from in this category, and which gaps are most actionable. The punch list orders the work, in priority sequence, with the technical specifics your team needs to execute.

We do not make the changes ourselves. No content writing. No website edits. No third-party profile updates. The labor stays with your team or your agency. We diagnose, we measure, and we hand the executing team a clear set of moves. That separation is not a limitation, it is the point. The agencies and tools that promise measurement and execution end up doing neither well, and the buyer never knows which lever produced the result.

06 What the first 30 days produces

The kickoff engagement establishes the baseline measurement and produces the first forensic readout. Concretely:

  • Query set design. We work with your team and your agency to lock the queries that actually route to ASB's commercial outcomes (mortgages, business banking, branch decisions, switching banks, "best Hawaii bank for...").
  • Three weeks of daily measurement across the seven engines on the locked query set. Three repetitions per query per day to control for noise.
  • Competitive cohort. Bank of Hawaii, First Hawaiian Bank, Central Pacific Bank, Hawaii USA Federal Credit Union, Hawaii State Federal Credit Union. The cohort can extend by request.
  • Source-type analysis. Which kinds of sources AI engines pull from for Hawaii banking. Are they citing third-party directories, news media, regulator filings, customer reviews, social, or the banks' own sites. The pattern shifts by engine and by category, and ASB's structural gaps are often visible here before they are visible in the citation count.
  • The research memo and punch list. Delivered to your team. Written for your team to act on. Forwardable to the agency that handles execution.

Ongoing measurement after the kickoff continues the daily capture and produces a monthly delta memo. Same shape, lighter weight, focused on what moved and why.

07 Brand authority signals, observed

Independent of the citation measurement, AI engines also lean on a set of off-site identity signals to decide which entities to surface in the first place. The snapshot below is a live look at ASB's authority profile as the engines see it today. Strong on some, missing on others. Not a citation predictor, but an honest read of where the brand is observable.

Each missing signal is a gap your team can close without us. We surface it. The execution stays yours.

08 Pricing

We charge per category, not per client. A category for ASB looks like "Hawaii consumer banking" or "Hawaii business banking" or "Hawaii mortgages." Each is its own engagement scope.

  • $4,500 kickoff per category. One time. Includes query set design, three weeks of daily measurement across the seven engines, the competitive cohort, source-type analysis, the research memo, and the prepped punch list for your executing team.
  • $1,500 per month per category, ongoing. Continued daily measurement, monthly delta memo, drift monitoring as the AI engines shift, and punch list refreshes as new gaps surface or close.

This is not a SaaS subscription. There is no self-serve dashboard. It is a research engagement, billed the way research engagements are billed, with the deliverable being a memo your team uses to act.

09 What we have done that is honest to claim

Hawaii Theatre Center, the historic Honolulu performing arts venue, has been a client across the company's pivot. The work we did for them, which is honest to describe and was substantively valuable, was diagnostic.

  • Surfaced an expired Charity Navigator profile (last updated 2023) that their prior SEO vendor had not flagged.
  • Surfaced a BBB profile that had not been touched since 1999.
  • Surfaced misconfigured authority backlinks to trusted institutions.
  • Surfaced the absence of a Bing Business Profile in a category where it matters.
  • Collaborated on meta description rewrites for the organization's primary entity description across the major engines.

These are the kinds of gaps a forensic measurement engagement surfaces that a standard SEO scan does not. None of them required us to ship code on the customer's property. The customer's team executed the fixes. We measure. They act. That is the shape of the work, and that is the shape an ASB engagement would take.

10 Questions you would ask, answered honestly

What changed about your product?
We used to ship a JavaScript snippet that we claimed would drive AI citations. We tested it against our own domain in a pre-registered kill test. The result was zero. The LLM crawlers do not execute JavaScript, which means the snippet was structurally invisible to them. We stopped selling that product, refunded what needed refunding, and rebuilt the company around the measurement layer that does work. The forensic readout described above is the actual product now.

Is this safe to bring into a regulated bank?
Yes, and by design. We do not touch your systems. No code on your property, no integration, no data flowing in from your side. We observe the public AI engines from the outside. This is a research engagement, not a software install. Your security review surface is an NDA and a vendor intake form, not a SOC 2 audit.

How long until citation share moves?
We do not predict citation lift. That was the failure mode of the prior product. What we promise is the measurement and the prepped guidance. The AI engines move on their own training cycles, the underlying ecosystem (Reddit partnerships, directory weighting, news source authority) shifts independently, and forecasting any specific lift in advance is selling theater. What we do tell you is exactly where you stand each week against every competitor on every engine on every query that matters. The execution your team or agency does against that knowledge is what moves the needle. We measure the movement.

How does this fit with the agencies and partners we already work with?
Cleanly. Your agency handles brand, creative, content production, paid media, and the relationships. We add the diagnostic and measurement layer on top. The punch list we hand your team or your agency is built for them to execute. They own the client outcome. We own the diagnostic layer they cannot credibly produce themselves because no platform will give it to them.

What is the relationship between AEO and SEO?
SEO is the byproduct of clean AEO work, not a separate billable. Schema clarity, authority signal integrity, profile freshness, and source-type targeting all show up in traditional search rankings too. We do not measure or bill SEO. We do not promise SEO outcomes. But we expect them to improve as a side effect, and we say so.

How is this different from Profound, Peec, or Athena HQ?
They sell brand dashboards with a score and a list of mentions, mostly to the brand directly, and quietly imply they will help with optimization too. We sell a research memo with a prepped punch list, to the brand or its agency, with the explicit boundary that we do not do the execution. The cross-engine cross-competitor view is the structural piece no single-platform self-dashboard can offer, because no platform owns the data on its competitors. That is the position only an outside observer can hold.

What do we own at the end?
Your memos, your punch lists, the raw data we captured against your queries. Everything is exportable. If ASB ever cancels, the data leaves with you. The category-level aggregate patterns we learn across our broader dataset stay with us, never named, never reverse-engineerable to a specific customer.

Can we see something concrete before committing?
Yes. We can run a single-day pilot scan against five sample queries before any commercial conversation, no charge. The output looks like the deliverable but at one-fifteenth the depth. It is enough to know whether the methodology surfaces something useful for ASB.

11 Why us, briefly and honestly
  • We have already retracted the thing we got wrong. Most vendors in adjacent categories have not. The retraction is the first sentence of this brief on purpose.
  • The codebase is open. The measurement tools are auditable. Your compliance team can read the same source code that produces the readout. github.com/lanceroylo/neverranked.
  • The five-query pilot is no-cost. Before any commitment, we run a five-query pilot on a banking category of your choosing so you see the shape of the deliverable on real ASB-adjacent data. It is not the full kickoff, but it is real, and it is free to see.
  • The company is small, founder-led, and Hawaii-based. That is the buying advantage. Direct access. No layer of account managers. The roadmap shapes around the customers who care.
12 Next step

A live walkthrough. About thirty minutes. I bring a real forensic readout from a comparable category so you can see the shape of the deliverable, walk through what an ASB engagement would produce, and decide together whether to scope a kickoff.
